title: Chapter 19: The Warmth of Black Flame
paragraphs: The wasteland breathed death. Ash, fine and prickly like ground glass, fell incessantly from the rotting skies, covering the dead earth in a thick, muffled carpet. On this night the wind did not howl—it moaned quietly, longingly, like a mourner over a mass grave.

The band of outcasts sat by a tiny campfire in the niche under the Etcha buttresses. After Lord Valthor's departure, the silence became even heavier. Dzhanavar slept, having placed his heavy wolf head on crossed paws, but his ears twitched sensitively, catching every rustle. Rene sat, hugging her knees, and looked at the smoldering embers, having forgotten to light a new cigarette. Kirah, curled into a huge resinous ball, sniffled quietly, hiding his muzzle from the cold.
Only Yashil was not sleeping. The goblin looked into the impenetrable gray gloom, where the light from their miserable campfire ended. He felt that this night had not yet given them all its pain.
First came a sound. A heavy, metallic rasp, rhythmic and frighteningly hopeless. As if someone were dragging an unbearable weight behind them.

The fur on Dzhanavar's scruff instantly stood on end. The werewolf opened his amber eyes, silently bared his teeth, and reached for his axe. Kirah opened his ruby eyes, drew in the frosty air with his nose, and a large, uncontrollable tremor ran through his giant body. The demon stepped back, pressing himself into the cold marble of the wall.
"The smell..." Kirah rasped, and in his voice sounded a primal, insurmountable horror. "The smell of ancient steel and absolute ash. It is death coming, Yashil. Death itself is coming."
From the gray mist, shuffling across the stones with iron-clad feet, a figure emerged.

It was a tall elf in heavy plate armor covered with centuries-old dents and dried black blood. But it was not the armor that made Rene drop her unlit cigarette; it was what crowned his head. Instead of hair, above a pale, sharpened, deathly-calm face, writhed an absolutely black flame. It gave no light. It greedily absorbed the dim reflections of their campfire, absorbing heat, pulling hope itself out of space.
But now this great, frightening flame was pitiful. It barely smoldered, convulsively shrinking, threatening to go out forever at any moment.

Avotu, the last apprentice of Sivarus, wandered blindly. His eyes—two bottomless pits in which night had forever frozen—looked through the outcasts, through the wall of Etcha, through time itself. He did not see before him the demon he was supposed to kill. He saw only the cool greenery of an ancient forest and Biotrix's hands slipping out of his fingers into the rift of the Void.

The fire elf took another unsteady step, tripped over a protruding root of a dead tree, and fell heavily, with a deafening clang of plate, to his knees right at the edge of the light from the campfire.

The Blade of Nature, the legendary sword forged from a meteorite, slipped from his weakened fingers with a ring and fell into the gray dust. The greatest warrior of the fading world lowered his head to his chest, his shoulders shook in soundless, dry agony. He did not cry—his tears had long since boiled away. He simply could not carry this weight anymore.
Dzhanavar froze, not even daring to breathe. The werewolf felt the power of this being in his gut, but now this power was directed inward, tearing the elf himself to pieces.
Yashil slowly rose.
"Master, no!" Kirah whispered in panic, trying to hold the goblin back with a huge claw. "That is the Black Flame. He burns everything that carries corruption. He will kill you."
Yashil softly but firmly set the demon's claw aside. He took a clean wooden bowl from his pack, scooped water into it from the flask, and stepped toward the fallen warrior.

The little goblin approached Avotu closely. From the elf wafted an unbearable, sepulchral cold. Yashil did not say a word. He simply knelt in the ash in front of the great warrior and held out the bowl of water to him.
Avotu slowly raised his head. His gaze, empty and terrible, focused on the tiny green figure.
"Why..." the elf's voice was like the rustling of dry autumn leaves under a boot. It sounded so quiet that Yashil had to lean forward. "Why do you hold out water to a dead man?"
"Dead men do not feel thirst, traveler," Yashil replied calmly. "And your lips are cracked from wind and ash. Drink."
Avotu did not move. He looked at the water as if it were poison.

"I did not hold her," suddenly the elf said in a cracked voice. This phrase burst out of him on its own, breaking the dam of century-old silence. "The skies shattered, and she disappeared into the darkness. I am the most powerful weapon of my era. My blade cleaves mountains. But when it was needed most, my hands proved absolutely, totally useless. I am the grave-digger of this world. And every breath of mine is an insult to her memory."
The black flame on his head painfully contracted, turning into pitiful, pulsing embers.
Rene turned her gaze away. The cynical fox felt physically pained to look at such naked, defenseless grief.
Yashil did not look away. He carefully placed the bowl of water on a stone between them.

"You blame yourself for staying alive," said the goblin. This sounded not as a reproach but as a statement of fact, spoken with infinite, understanding softness. "You think that your grief is a punishment for weakness."
Avotu convulsively drew in the frosty air. "And is it not? If I had been faster... If I had been stronger..."

"Power has no sway over the decay of the fabric of the universe," Yashil shook his head. "No blade can cleave that which has no form. You did not lose a battle, Avotu. You simply faced inevitability."

The goblin held out his small, calloused green hand and, breaking all conceivable laws of this world, cautiously touched the icy, steel-clad plate gauntlet of the elf. Avotu flinched at the touch, but did not pull his hand away.

"My mother pushed me into a drainage pipe and stayed to face the fire so that I might live," Yashil continued quietly, looking into the bottomless eyes of the elf. "She was burned in our village during the High Elf raid. For a long time I thought my life was a curse. That I should have died with her. But then I realized one thing. The grief we carry within us is not a punishment. It is the only place where those we lost continue to live."
Yashil pointed to the fading black flame on Avotu's head.

"Your flame is black not because you are evil. It is black because it has absorbed all the pain of those you loved. You carry them within you. If you now allow this flame to go out... if you lie down in this ash and surrender... that is when she truly dies forever. Because the last one who remembers the warmth of her hands will vanish."

The goblin's words fell into Avotu's dead, scorched soul like heavy, life-giving drops of rain on a parched desert. Centuries of self-torture, cold despair, and self-hatred collided with a simple, disarming truth.
His grief is not guilt. It is his love, which has nowhere else to go.
Avotu closed his eyes. Not a tear flowed from under his tightly closed eyelids, but his broad shoulders slowly, with a painful creak of plate, straightened. A deep, heavy breath filled his lungs.

He opened his eyes. The emptiness in them had not vanished, but it had ceased to be dead. It became deep and calm, like the night sky before dawn. The black flame above his pale face flickered. It did not change color, and no golden spark appeared—it remained a dark, cold fire, but it stopped devouring its host. For the first time in centuries, the flame burned steady.

Slowly, with a trembling hand, the great warrior reached for the bowl of water. He brought it to his parched lips and took a sip. The water was icy, with a taste of clay, but for Avotu it was the taste of returning to the path.

"You cannot bring her back from the darkness, traveler," Yashil finished in a whisper, putting away the empty bowl. "But you can become a shield for those who still hold someone by the hand in this rotting world. So that they do not have to go through what you went through."

Avotu lowered his hand to the hilt of the Blade of Nature. The weapon no longer hummed with a destructive storm. It responded with a quiet, devoted vibration. The elf slowly rose from his knees. His height was overwhelming, his aura remained frightening, but now there was a nascent purpose in it.
He looked at Yashil. At the tiny green-skinned outcast who had just done what the thousand-year-old folios and the magic of the ancients could not do. He had given him a reason to keep breathing.
Avotu slowly, with deep reverence, bowed his head to the goblin. It was not a nod of politeness. It was an acknowledgment of one soul to another.

"I am Avotu," the elf spoke, and his voice regained the hardness of basalt. "I will remember your words, Yashil of the Rotten Hollow. I will take them with me. And while my heart beats, I will try to be that shield."

Avotu turned around. He did not approach the campfire. He stepped back into the gray, frozen mist of the wastelands, carrying within himself the wisdom of a tiny goblin. He was still broken, still burdened by the past, but he was walking.

Dzhanavar, watching all this with an open maw, quietly clicked his teeth and exhaled noisily. "Augh..." the werewolf drawled with reverence. "Brother... you just gave a lecture to the god of death, and he took notes for you. I officially recognize: you are the coolest predator in this pack."

Rene silently took a silver lighter from her pocket, flicked the wheel, and lit a new cigarette. Her hands were shaking slightly. "Perhaps," the fox said quietly, looking in the direction where Avotu had vanished, "in this rotting world there is still reason to linger a little longer."

None of them yet knew that this meeting on the edge of the abyss would forever change Avotu's fate, preparing him for the day when five simple, terrified people from another world would fall to him from torn space. But that would be later.
Meanwhile, Yashil simply sat back down by the fire, holding out his green, calloused hands to the dim embers.
